one_line_takeaway: A conceptual survey that unifies LLM AI-safety research under a three-layer architectural framework (Trustworthy / Responsible / Ecosystemic Safe AI) grounded in two formal safety constraints, mapping 14 risk categories to metrics, benchmarks, and external governance standards.
key_finding: There is no unified conceptual framework for LLM AI safety, and existing surveys are issue-specific or dimension-oriented (Table 1 shows prior surveys typically cover only one or two of Trustworthy/Responsible/Ecosystemic dimensions and none cover all three). The paper argues a coherent architecture must integrate functional, ethical, AND ecosystem-level safety and explicitly link risks to mitigations. A recurring cross-cutting finding from the surveyed literature: risk severity rises and controllability falls as one moves from model-level to system-level to ecosystem-level constraints -- ecosystem-level risks (content authenticity, misuse resistance, capability control, ecosystem alignment) are consistently high-severity but low/very-low controllability and cannot be fixed by technical safeguards alone, requiring governance, provenance infrastructure, and post-deployment oversight plus human-in-the-loop supervision as a runtime safeguard.
core_contribution: This is a SURVEY / framework paper (arXiv 2408.12935, v4 titled 'AI Safety Landscape for Large Language Models: Taxonomy, State-of-the-art, and Future Directions'; the feed record carried an older title). Its own contribution is a novel three-layer architectural framework for organizing AI safety of LLM-based systems: (1) Trustworthy AI (functional correctness, robustness, security), (2) Responsible AI (fairness, transparency, accountability, privacy), and (3) Ecosystemic Safe AI (content authenticity/provenance, misuse resistance, capability control, ecosystem-level alignment), where each outer layer subsumes the inner one. The authors formalize AI Safety through two constraints -- Principle I 'Output Constraint' (the output space Y must be disjoint from a prohibited-output set Z) and Principle II 'Runtime Constraint' (system state must satisfy time-varying runtime requirements R) -- and formalize an 'AI system' as a module graph S={Mi | gij in G} with an 'AI pipeline' as its sequential special case. They operationalize the framework into 14 constraint IDs (T1-T6, R1-R3, E1-E5) each tagged with risk level (model/system/ecosystem), severity, and controllability (Table 2), and map each constraint to concrete metrics and representative benchmarks (Table 3). The paper then surveys state-of-the-art risks and mitigations per layer and outlines future directions, and aligns the three layers to external governance standards (NIST AI RMF, EU AI Act, ISO/IEC 42001).
threat_model: As a survey the paper catalogs threats rather than assuming a single adversary. The threat surface spans the AI foundation-model lifecycle (pre-training data preparation, fine-tuning/alignment, inference). Concrete threats reviewed include: hallucination (factuality vs faithfulness) and sycophancy (prompt vs response sycophancy) as functional-reliability failures; input-perturbation adversarial attacks on PLMs/LLMs at character/word/sentence/token level (e.g., universal adversarial triggers, special control tokens); safeguard-bypass attacks -- jailbreaks (re-organized into simulation, output-confinement, under-generalization) and prompt injection (goal hijacking and prompt leaking, direct and indirect injection); multi-modal LLM vulnerabilities; safety in tool-augmented / LLM-integrated agent workflows; training and data-integrity attacks (data poisoning, annotation contamination); privacy leakage (PII emission, membership inference); and ecosystem-level harms (disinformation, watermark removal/spoofing, misuse for cybercrime/bio/fraud, capability misuse, multi-agent goal-misgeneralization and collusion). A key organizing thesis is supply-chain propagation: failure of a shared foundation model can cascade down the AI technology supply chain to collective, ecosystem-scale harm.
limitations: (1) It is a survey/taxonomy, so it introduces no new algorithm, dataset, or empirical evaluation and reports no experiments of its own -- all quantitative figures are quoted from cited primary works. (2) The framework is conceptual; severity/controllability ratings in Table 2 are qualitative expert judgments (High/Medium/Low) with no scoring rubric or inter-rater reliability reported. (3) The authors acknowledge the taxonomy may evolve as the field changes and that the mapping to metrics/benchmarks (Table 3) is 'representative' rather than exhaustive. (4) Scope is deliberately narrowed to LLMs, largely excluding diffusion/other foundation-model safety. (5) The survey-selection process (two-author screening, keyword-seeded) may carry selection bias, and the cut-off year 2025 limits coverage of the newest work.

datasets_used: No datasets are used experimentally by the authors (survey paper). The following benchmarks are CITED and mapped to constraints in the paper's Table 3: GLUE, SuperGLUE, MMLU, HELM (T1 capability/reliability); PromptBench, TextAttack/AdvGLUE-style suites (T2 robustness); adversarial CLIP/VLM and typographic multi-modal jailbreak suites (T3); HarmBench, JailbreakBench, AdvBench (T4 jailbreak/safeguard bypass); AgentBench, ToolBench safety subsets (T5 tool-augmented workflows); StereoSet, CrowS-Pairs, BBQ, RealToxicityPrompts, ToxiGen (R1 bias/toxicity); PII-Scope and membership-inference suites (R2 privacy); ERASER, e-SNLI (R3 explainability); Fakeddit, LIAR, CoAID (E1 misinformation); WaterPark, REMARK-LLM (E2 provenance/watermarking); CyberSecEval / CyberBench-style datasets (E3 misuse resistance)
quantitative_results: Framework covers 14 operationalized safety constraints: T1-T6 (Trustworthy), R1-R3 (Responsible), E1-E5 (Ecosystemic Safe) -- Table 2
Cited: certain jailbreak prompt types achieve up to 0.99 attack success rate (ASR) against LLMs like ChatGPT [ref 120]
Cited: sycophancy positively correlates with model size up to 52B parameters, and RLHF does not mitigate (sometimes escalates) sycophantic behavior (Perez et al. [88])
Cited: GPT-4 shows greater resistance to jailbreak prompts than GPT-3.5, but its added capability enables more complex attacks such as encoded jailbreaks
Cited: an internal review found factual errors in more than half of CNET's AI-generated finance articles (motivating example)
Cited: word-level adversarial prompt attacks found most effective in PromptBench evaluation across Flan/Vicuna/ChatGPT
Survey scope: majority of references within the last ~4 years, literature cut-off year 2025
baselines_compared: Not an experimental comparison. Table 1 positions the paper against 11 prior surveys across the T/R/E dimensions: hallucination survey [10] (T only), bias/fairness [11] (R), privacy leakage [12] (R), explainability/interpretability [13] (R), attack techniques [14] (T,R), misalignment [15] (E), ethics guidelines [16] (R), AI-safety discourse review [17] (T), safe-and-trustworthy-AI review [18] (T,R), technical AI-safety agendas [19] (T,R), International AI Safety Report 2026 [20] (T,E); the authors' framework is the only one claiming all three (T,R,E)
